div.p: ok so im building an Mpesa phishing detector and i want to do sm really unique coz im training Ai with Td idf and naive bayes and iwanna make a stream lit so today is day 21of the hackathon we js getting to learn abit abt cyber sec

div.p: That's a dope project! 🔥 Day 21 of a cybersec hackathon and you're building an ML-powered Mpesa phishing detector — that's actually really practical given how rampant Mpesa scams are in Kenya.
So you've got TF-IDF + Naive Bayes for the model, and Streamlit for the frontend. Smart combo — fast to build, interpretable results.
What's the "unique" angle you're going for? A few ideas to spark something:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Show the top TF-IDF words that triggered the detection (explainability is huge)
Confidence score with a visual risk meter
Compare Naive Bayes vs a second model live

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Paste an SMS and get instant analysis
Highlight the suspicious words in red directly in the message
A "why is this phishing?" explanation panel

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Train on real Mpesa scam patterns (fake paybill numbers, urgency language like "Mwisho wa siku", sender spoofing cues)

div.p: What does your dataset look like right now — did you collect real Mpesa SMS samples, or are you working with something synthetic? And what's your current model accuracy sitting at?

div.p: 18 tasks across 4 phases — tap to check things off as you go! 🔥

The unique angle that'll make yours stand out is the keyword highlighting + "why is this phishing?" explainability panel in phase 3 — most people just show a label, but showing which words triggered it is way more impressive and actually useful.
What do you want to tackle first — collecting data or setting up the project structure?

div.p: DAY 1 Ignition Day Orientation, rules briefing, and 3 hands-on workshops covering banking cybersecurity, AI fraud detection, and encryption. Teams form, declare their challenge track and build mode, then kick off their first build sprint. You leave Day 1 with your plan locked in and your team fired up. �� DAY 2 Deep Build Day A full uninterrupted day of building with mentors circulating the floor throughout. No workshops — just pure focused build time. Aim to have your core functionality working by end of Day 2. DAY 3 Pitch & Awards Day Final build window closes at 11:00 AM — code freeze! Teams set up their demo stations, pitch live to the judges panel (3 min pitch + 2 min demo + 2 min Q&A), and we close with the awards ceremony and group

div.p: Oh so this is a 3-day hackathon! Not 21 days 😂 Day 1 is today and you're already planning — that's the right energy.
So the timeline is tight:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Day 1 (today) — plan locked, team formed, start building
Day 2 (tomorrow) — core functionality done by end of day
Day 3 — code freeze at 11 AM, then pitch + demo

div.p: That changes everything. You need to be ruthless about scope. For your Mpesa phishing detector, by end of Day 2 you need: model trained, Streamlit app running, demo-ready.
A few quick questions to help you prioritize:

Q: Are you building solo or with a team? A: Solo

div.p: Ok solo + no dataset — let's be strategic about this. You don't have time to collect real SMS samples from scratch, so here's the move:
For the dataset, use a combo approach:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Kaggle — search "SMS spam dataset" — the UCI SMS Spam Collection has 5,500+ messages. Not Mpesa-specific but good enough to train on, and you can add a few Mpesa-flavored samples manually

Manually write ~20-30 Mpesa phishing examples — you literally know what they look like ("Umeshinda KSh 50,000! Tuma KSh 200 activation fee to paybill..."), this takes 30 mins and makes your model locally relevant

div.p: This gives you a working dataset by tonight.

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Tonight (Day 1) — dataset ready + model training done
Day 2 morning — Streamlit app built and functional
Day 2 afternoon — explainability features + polish
Day 3 before 11 AM — rehearse your 3 min pitch

div.p: The 3-min pitch is huge as a solo — judges will respect the hustle but you need a tight story: "Mpesa fraud costs Kenyans X billion yearly. I built a real-time SMS detector using ML that flags phishing with Y% accuracy."
Want me to help you write the synthetic Mpesa phishing SMS samples right now so you can get your dataset going? 🚀

div.p: Love the energy! It's only Day 1 and you're already ahead. Let's use this time to get a serious head start. Here's what would actually earn you extra points with judges:

p.strong: The highest impact things you can do right now:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Start building the model tonight — don't wait for Day 2. If your dataset is ready, open Jupyter and get TF-IDF + Naive Bayes running. Walking into Day 2 with a working model is a massive advantage.

Make your dataset more Kenyan — the UCI dataset has no Mpesa, no Safaricom language, no "Umeshinda", no paybill scams. Spend 30 mins writing authentic local phishing SMS samples. Judges will notice the local context.
Sketch your Streamlit UI on paper — plan exactly what screens you want before you code. Saves hours tomorrow.
Prepare your demo story — judges love a real scenario. Think of a character: "Wanjiku gets this SMS..." and build your demo around her. Way more memorable than just pasting text.
Research your problem statement — Google "Mpesa fraud Kenya statistics 2024", find a real number (like KSh X billion lost to fraud). That one stat in your pitch opening hits hard.

div.p: What do you want to tackle right now — should we start writing the dataset, or jump straight into the model code? 🔥

div.p: Great question — and the short answer is: yes it can, but it's easy to fix.

With Naive Bayes, the order itself doesn't matter mathematically. But there's a real problem called data leakage from ordering — if your CSV has all phishing messages first and all legit messages last, and you do a simple train/test split without shuffling, your model trains on mostly phishing and tests on mostly legit. That gives you garbage accuracy metrics and a biased model.
The fix is one line in sklearn:

absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

div.p: shuffle=True is actually the default, so you're likely already fine — but worth knowing why it matters.
Also worth doing is this before the split:

div.p: That shuffles the whole dataframe upfront so everything downstream is clean.

div.p: This is actually really solid for Day 1! The code is clean, well-commented, and has good structure. The session state, caching, history table — all the right things are there.
Here's my honest breakdown:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Sample messages are a great demo touch
The sidebar dashboard with fraud rate is smart
The "What should I do?" expander is genuinely useful
Swahili keywords show local context — judges will love that

p.strong: What would make it look more professional:

div.p: The biggest issue right now is that Streamlit's default look is very "out of the box". A few targeted changes will make it look like you actually designed it:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Add a custom theme — create a .streamlit/config.toml file. This alone transforms the whole app's feel instantly.
The sample message buttons are broken — your current code creates new column sets each time with st.columns(4)[0] instead of reusing the first set. The buttons will appear stacked, not side by side.
The result cards look flat — using st.markdown() with custom HTML for the verdict (instead of st.error/st.success) makes it look way more polished.
No logo or branding — even a simple shield icon with "PhishGuard KE" or something Kenyan-branded makes it feel like a real product.
The history table has no color coding — phishing rows should be red, legit should be green.

div.p: Want me to write you the fixes for all of these? The theme file + the button fix + the branded result card would take your UI from "student project" to "product demo" in like 20 minutes. 🔥

div.p: Yes absolutely! There are a few ways to do it:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Go to canva.com → click "Create a design" → search "Logo" → use their AI logo generator. Free, takes 2 mins, downloads as PNG.
Or looka.com — type your app name, pick a style, generates a proper logo. Free to preview.
Or just ask me — I can generate an SVG logo right here that you can save and drop straight into your Streamlit app as an image file. No external site needed.

div.p: Option 2 — Use it in Streamlit Once you have a logo file (logo.png), you use it like this:

div.p: Here's a prompt you can paste into any AI image generator (Canva AI, Adobe Firefly, DALL-E, or Ideogram):

blockquote.p: Minimalist tech logo for a cybersecurity app called "PhishGuard KE". A sleek shield with a subtle M-PESA green accent and a checkmark or signal wave inside. Flat design, no gradients, transparent background, professional, modern fintech aesthetic.

p.strong: Detailed version (for DALL-E / Firefly / Ideogram — better results):

blockquote.p: A clean, minimalist logo for a mobile security app called "PhishGuard KE". The icon is a modern flat shield shape with a digital fingerprint or wifi signal inside it. Color palette: deep navy blue (#1B2A4A) as the primary color with M-PESA green (#00A651) as the accent. The text "PhishGuard" in bold sans-serif underneath with "KE" in the green accent color. Transparent background, vector style, no shadows, no gradients, suitable for both light and dark backgrounds.

hr.p: Best site to use this on: ideogram.ai — it's genuinely the best free tool for logos with text right now. Canva AI tends to mess up the text spelling.
Want me to just build you the SVG logo directly here instead? I can have it ready in 30 seconds and it'll be pixel-perfect. 👀

div.p: Here's exactly how to add the logo, plus a full UI upgrade plan:
First, save the SVG. I'll give you the file:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Download that, put it in an assets/ folder inside your project. Then replace your current header section with this:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

p.strong: Tweaks that will genuinely impress judges:

hr.p: 1. Custom theme — biggest bang for zero effort. Create .streamlit/config.toml in your project root:
